paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number
summary: Conjecture 6 from arXiv:1808.01605, asserting that high chromatic number forces a subgraph of girth at least g with average degree at least k for all k and g, remains open in full generality. The source paper itself establishes the g=4 case (triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number), and the g=3 case is trivial via the degeneracy bound. No follow-up paper resolving or substantially advancing the g≥5 cases was found in a targeted web search through 2026.

internal_refs_verified.note: Paper extends Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs; addresses different host graphs (digraphs) and a different structural parameter. Not directly relevant to the undirected chromatic-number/girth/average-degree conjecture.
Paper concerns Hadwiger's conjecture and clique minors in chromatic-critical graphs; unrelated to Conjecture 6.
Paper proves clique-minor bounds in K_s-free graphs; unrelated to Conjecture 6.

WebFetch of abstract page returned insufficient content for confirmation. Based on the corpus-extracted contribution (constructing graphs with bounded Hall ratio and large fractional chromatic number), the paper addresses a different open problem attributed to Dvořák, Ossona de Mendez, and Wu — not Conjecture 6 from 1808.01605.

search_queries: chromatic number girth average degree subgraph conjecture Mohar Wu 1808.01605
Thomassen conjecture chromatic number girth large average degree subgraph progress 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
high chromatic number subgraph large girth large average degree conjecture Mohar Wu graph theory 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025
notes: No follow-up found in indexed literature. The conjecture is a weakening of the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture (using chromatic number as hypothesis and requiring only large average degree, not large chromatic number, in the subgraph). The g=3 case is trivially true by degeneracy; the g=4 case is proved in the source paper. The Keevash–Li–Mohar preprint 'Digraph girth via chromatic number' (Mohar is a co-author) appeared in search results but treats directed graphs and could not be read in full. arXiv:2605.02543 (2026) proves a different variant of Thomassen's conjecture (highly connected subgraphs with large chromatic number), not girth/average-degree. Confidence is medium rather than high because the conjecture is 7 years old and absence of indexed evidence for an open problem of this age carries moderate uncertainty.
